agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver 2025 Homeless Count results presented to Council
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a report and presentation on the 2025 Homeless Count for Vancouver. This count provides a snapshot of homelessness in the city, including the number of people experiencing homelessness and key trends. While no immediate bylaw or rule change is proposed, the findings could influence future city policies, funding decisions, and support programs that may affect local businesses, especially those in the downtown and commercial areas.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.	2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
Presentation
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 Cultural Grants for Operating & Spaces
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed 2026 Cultural Grants that would provide funding for arts and culture organizations in Burnaby. The grants cover two areas: operating costs and cultural spaces. Because some of the recommendations require a special two-thirds majority vote under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter (a rule that demands extra support from council members for certain decisions), this is not a done deal yet. The decision is still upcoming, and the outcome depends on the council vote.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote on these grants — if approved, they could affect funding for your arts or cultural organization.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.	2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces)
Recommendations A to C, E and G require at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants for Spring 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to award grants to community sport events happening in Vancouver during the Spring of 2026. This is part of the Vancouver Community Sport Event Grant program. The recommendation requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote 'yes' to pass, as per Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter (a special rule for certain council decisions). If approved, the grants would help local organizations host sporting events in the city.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you run a community sport event in Vancouver, watch for the council decision — this could mean funding opportunities for your Spring 2026 event.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
3.	Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake
Recommendation A requires at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby property tax distribution for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on how to divide the total property tax levy among different classes of properties in Burnaby for 2026. This proposed decision will determine what share of the city's property tax bill is paid by homeowners, businesses, and other property types. A 'property tax levy' is the total amount of money the city needs to collect from property taxes. The proposed distribution sets the tax rate for each class. If approved, this will directly affect how much your business pays in property taxes next year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this will set your 2026 property tax rate.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
4.	2026 Property Taxation: Distribution of Property Tax Levy
4a. Budget Resolution
4b. Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 property tax exemptions for non-profits
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a report that recommends granting property tax exemptions for 2025 to certain non-profit organizations in Burnaby. A property tax exemption means the organization does not have to pay property tax on the land or building it owns or leases. This decision would affect which groups get a break on their taxes next year. The report lists the specific organizations being considered for the exemption. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on the proposal.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you represent a non-profit on the list, watch for the council decision to confirm your exemption. If you are not on the list, contact the city to ask about applying.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
5.	2025 Property Tax Exemptions
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Coastal Flood Management Policy
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed Coastal Flood Management Policy. This policy would create new rules for building and development in areas of Burnaby that are at risk of flooding from the ocean. If approved, property owners in these flood-risk zones may face new requirements for building elevations, flood-proofing, and insurance. The goal is to protect people and property from rising sea levels and storm surges. The policy is still a proposal and has not been finalized.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in a proposed flood-risk zone and review the draft policy before the council vote.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Insurance
agenda_briefs.source_body:
6.	Coastal Flood Management Policy
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider Vancouver Heritage Foundation annual report and board reappointment
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a recommendation to reappoint members to the Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board. This recommendation requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote in favour, as stated under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter. The decision is not yet final — it will be decided at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.	Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board – Annual Report 2025
Recommendation B requires at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of theVancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on public plaza alcohol sites for 2026-2029
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed program that would allow people to drink alcohol in certain public plazas in Burnaby from 2026 to 2029. This is not a final decision yet — the report is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting. If approved, the program would designate specific plaza locations where adults could consume alcohol in public, similar to existing pilot programs in other cities. The proposal includes rules about times, locations, and how the program would be managed. Business owners near potential plaza sites should pay attention, as this could affect foot traffic, noise levels, and local business operations.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider contacting your city councillor if you have concerns or support for the proposed plaza locations.

key_dates.label: Burnaby public plaza alcohol program council vote

agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.	Sites for 2026-2029 Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to apply for development approvals funding
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on whether to apply for a provincial grant through the Local Government Development Approvals Program. If approved, the funding would help the city speed up its review and approval process for new development projects. This could mean faster decisions on building permits and rezoning applications for local businesses and developers. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on the application at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, this could speed up your future development applications in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.	Funding Application to Local Government Development Approvals Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on housing rezoning rule changes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a package of miscellaneous amendments that would change the conditions attached to various housing rezoning approvals in Burnaby. These proposed changes could affect what developers and property owners must do — such as providing community amenities, affordable units, or public benefits — before getting final approval to build. The exact details of which rezoning conditions would be altered are not yet public, but the decision could impact current and future housing projects. A report on the amendments is on the agenda, but no public speakers are allowed at this stage under council's procedure rules.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision and any public hearing dates — this could affect your development project's conditions.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
10.	Miscellaneous Amendments Concerning Various Housing Rezoning Conditions
(no speakers per section 7.2(b) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Text amendment to CD-1 (839) bylaw for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed text amendment to the Comprehensive Development (CD-1) Bylaw No. 13493 for two properties at 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. A CD-1 bylaw is a special zoning rule that allows a specific development project to have different rules than the usual zoning. This amendment would change the written rules (the 'text') of that bylaw, not the map or boundaries. The exact changes are described in the draft bylaw included in the council agenda. This is a proposed change only — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date — if approved, this could affect development rules for these properties.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.	CD-1 (839) Text Amendment: 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue
(no speakers per section 7.2(b) of the Procedure By-law)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning at 4088 Granville Street, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone 4088 Granville Street. A 'CD-1' (Comprehensive Development) rezoning is a special zoning category that allows a specific development plan for a property, rather than following the standard rules for that area. This is a first step — the draft bylaw is up for approval at the next council meeting. If passed, it would change what can be built on this site.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date — this rezoning could affect nearby property values or development plans.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

(no speakers per section 7.2(b) of the Procedure By-law)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning for 3803-3823 West 10th Ave and 2553 Highbury St
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for properties at 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street. If approved, the project would change the land-use rules (called a CD-1 rezoning) to allow a new development on these sites. The proposal also includes changes to the city's sign bylaw. This is not yet approved — it is up for a council decision at an upcoming meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning for 310 East 14th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the property at 310 East 14th Avenue. This is called a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) rezoning, which means the city would create a special set of rules just for this site, instead of following the standard zoning rules. The draft bylaw would amend Burnaby's Zoning and Development Bylaw. A decision has not been made yet — this item is on the council meeting agenda for discussion and a vote.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this rezoning could affect nearby property values and development plans.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the properties at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue. A 'rezoning' means changing the official rules about what can be built on that land. The draft bylaw would amend the city's Zoning and Development Bylaw. This is a proposal only — it has not been approved yet. Council will consider the draft bylaw at an upcoming meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to consult on new development cost charges
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a report on the 2026 Financing Growth Update. This includes proposed changes to the Amenity Cost Charge Bylaw and updates to Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning rules under Bill 16. These are tools the city uses to make developers pay for community amenities (like parks and community centres) and affordable housing when they build new projects. The report outlines a required public consultation process before any new bylaw is passed. A decision has not been made yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public consultation dates — this is your chance to have a say on new costs for development projects.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
16.	2026 Financing Growth Update – Required Consultation for Amenity Cost Charge By law and Bill 16 Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning Updates
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Trounce Alley & Blood Alley Square maintenance assessment bylaw
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge property owners in the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square area for the 2025 maintenance costs of those public spaces. This is a 'local improvement' project — a special assessment where the city pays for the work upfront and then bills the property owners who benefit directly. The bylaw would create a legal way to collect those maintenance fees from the affected properties. This is not yet approved; Council will consider it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in the Trounce Alley or Blood Alley Square assessment area — if so, watch for the council decision date.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Food Service
agenda_briefs.source_body: 8.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 maintenance costs for the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square Local Improvement Project
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw to assess parking costs on East Hastings
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge property owners along East Hastings Street to help pay for the 2025 costs of the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project. A 'bylaw to assess real property' means the city would add a special fee to certain properties to cover the cost of a shared parking project. This is not yet approved — it is up for discussion and a vote at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending or submitting comments if you own property on East Hastings Street.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 7.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 costs for the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby housing agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would create a Housing Agreement for the property at 320-360 West 2nd Avenue. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that locks in how the building will be used — for example, requiring a certain number of affordable rental units. This decision has not been made yet. If approved, the agreement would set rules for the site, likely affecting how many rental units must be kept affordable and for how long.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect development plans or rental rules for this property.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 6.	A By-law to enact a Housing Agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Amendment to housing agreement for Prior Street, Malkin Avenue, Station Street
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would amend an existing Housing Agreement for properties at 450-496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue, and 1002 Station Street. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that sets rules for how the housing is used — for example, keeping rents affordable or ensuring units stay as rental housing. This amendment would change the terms of that existing agreement. The decision has not been made yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 5.	A By-law to authorize the amendment of a Housing Agreement Authorized by By-law No. 14507 (450 – 496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue and 1002 Station Street)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 rezoning for 888 West 8th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for the property at 888 West 8th Avenue. The owner wants to change the land-use designation to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone. A CD-1 zone is a custom zoning category that allows a specific development plan, rather than following the standard rules for that area. This proposal would allow a new mixed-use building with commercial space on the ground floor and residential units above. Council will consider the referral report at an upcoming meeting. If approved, the project would move to a public hearing where residents and business owners can share their views.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to comment on the proposed rezoning.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Fast-track rental housing and small hotels in new Burnaby commercial district
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposal to create a new 'Mixed-Use Commercial District' that would fast-track approvals for rental housing and small hotels. If approved, this new zoning category would allow developers to build rental apartments and small hotels (under a certain size) more quickly, without needing a full rezoning process each time. The goal is to speed up the construction of much-needed rental homes and tourist accommodations. Council will consider the referral report at an upcoming meeting — this is not yet law.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending or submitting comments if you own land or a hotel in the proposed district.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Landlord & Property Mgmt

Referral Report
